coredata.dc:description: The objective of this study isto develop novel and sustainable corrosion inhibitors, which is an important and challenging task in corrosion management. This study reports thefabrication of a novel nanocomposite (NC) through the functionalization of an iron metal-organic framework (MOF) using cumin-derived carbon dots (CD). The MOF@CD NCwas tested as a novel corrosion inhibitor for Q235B steel in 1.0 M HCl solution. The corrosion inhibition potential was significantly improved through the functionalization process and gravimetric tests showed an inhibition efficiency of about 98 % after 72 h immersion at 25 °C with an inhibitor concentration of 100 mg/L. FT-IR and XPS analysis indicated that binding and adsorption were achieved through functional groups of MOF@CD NC. SEM and AFM studies showed significant surface damage reduction and supported the formation of apowerful protective layer in the presence of inhibitor. This research represents a significant step forward in corrosion protection technology by introducing a new class of materials with excellent performance at low concentrations, offering a promising solution for industrial applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The objective of this study isto develop novel and sustainable corrosion inhibitors, which is an important and challenging task in corrosion management. This study reports thefabrication of a novel nanocomposite (NC) through the functionalization of an iron metal-organic framework (MOF) using cumin-derived carbon dots (CD). The MOF@CD NCwas tested as a novel corrosion inhibitor for Q235B steel in 1.0 M HCl solution. The corrosion inhibition potential was significantly improved through the functionalization process and gravimetric tests showed an inhibition efficiency of about 98 % after 72 h immersion at 25 °C with an inhibitor concentration of 100 mg/L. FT-IR and XPS analysis indicated that binding and adsorption were achieved through functional groups of MOF@CD NC. SEM and AFM studies showed significant surface damage reduction and supported the formation of apowerful protective layer in the presence of inhibitor. This research represents a significant step forward in corrosion protection technology by introducing a new class of materials with excellent performance at low concentrations, offering a promising solution for industrial applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Pipelines have become an integral and economical means of efficiently transporting crude oil and natural gas, with high operational speed and large transport capacity [1]. Pipelines are usually made from carbon steel because of its low cost and good mechanical properties. Nevertheless, thesepipelines are prone to corrosion in aggressive media such as saline water, acidic medium, and microbes [2,3]. Internal pipeline corrosion is a vital issue that can causesignificant economic loss and safety hazards [4]. However, the reliance on these pipelines hasincreased with growing energy demands and fast-paced industrialization, exposing them to harsh environmental conditions such as high salinity in coastal areas, acidic soils and industrial toxins [5]. Corrosion is a severe and persistent concern, with the National Association of Corrosion Engineers (NACE) estimating the global cost of corrosion at $2.5 trillion (3.4 % of the world’s GDP) [6]. Pipeline corrosion can have severe consequences, including environmental damage, safety risks, and operational disruptions [7]. Tackling these constraints requires innovative and sustainable solutions. An economical method for mitigating pipeline internal corrosion is to continuously inject corrosioninhibitors into the fluid stream at parts-per-million concentrations [8]. Inhibitors, in contrast, adsorb onto the metal surfaceand effectively create a barrier to water and corrosive agents. The effectiveness of this protection relies on the inhibitor's ability to create a uniform, adherent layer facilitated by polar functional groups and intermolecular interactions [8,9]. Inhibitors adsorb onto metal surfaces through physisorption or chemisorption. Physisorption occurs via weak electrostatic andvan der Waals forces. In contrast,chemisorption produces more robust bonds via conjugative or π-electron sharing with the d-orbitals of the metal. Imidazolines, phosphate esters, and thiols containnitrogen, phosphorus, oxygen, or sulfur functional groups that are the adsorption centers at a molecular level [10,11]. Although organic inhibitors are widely applied, their synthesis frequently contradicts the principles of green chemistry and can have negative effects on the environment [10,12]. This has spurred research and development of eco-friendly,non-toxic and biodegradable corrosion inhibitors. New progress in the field of green inhibitors enables efficient corrosion protection with a lower environmental footprint.

Green inhibitors have garnered significant interest butstill suffer from low efficiency. High-performance environmentally sustainable inhibitors are a critical research focus. In this context, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) represent a potential solution. MOFs are composed of metal ions or clusters that are connected byorganic ligands to form highly ordered crystalline structures [13,14]. MOFs have garnered significant attention as corrosion inhibitors owing to their high surface area, tunable porosity, and diverse chemical functionalities. Although only a limited number of studies have explored their use in corrosion protection, existing reports highlight their promising potential. For instance, Mesbah et al. [15] investigated Mg-based MOFs (Mg(C7)₂ and Mg(C10)₂) for corrosion inhibition of magnesium in neutral media. Fouda et al. [16] reported Cu-based MOFs derived from quinoxalinecarboxylic acid and pyrazinecarboxylic acid for copper corrosion protection in HCl, achieving up to 83 % inhibition efficiency at 300 ppm. Etaiw et al. [17] developed a Cd-based MOF using cadmium thiocyanate for effective corrosion inhibition of copper in HCl. Zafari et al. [18] examined a ZIF-8@{Mo₁₃₂} hybrid MOF for carbon steel protection in HCl, where 700 ppm of the inhibitor exhibited 92 % efficiency after 10 h of immersion. Mandal et al. [19] studied Cd- and Zn-based MOFs as corrosion inhibitors for mild steel in 3.5 wt% NaCl, achieving 77 % and 90 % inhibition efficiencies, respectively, at a concentration of 50 ppm. Anadebe et al. [20] evaluated a Co-MOF (ZIF-67) for CO₂-induced corrosion of X65 steel and investigated a Ce-MOF for corrosion inhibition of copper alloys in chloride-rich media [21], with inhibition efficiencies of 95 % and 81 %, respectively, at 0.1 % concentration. Despite these encouraging findings, conventional powdered MOFs face inherent limitations such as poor solubility, weak adhesion to metal surfaces, and inconsistent inhibition performance. To overcome these drawbacks, modified MOFs [22] achieved through strategies such as functionalization, heteroatom doping, or nanoparticle incorporation have emerged as a viable solution. These modifications enhance surface interactions, provide additional active sites, and significantly improve corrosion protection efficiency.

Modified MOFs can be designed to be biodegradable or synthesized from renewable resources, reducing their ecological impact [23]. The incorporation of other materials in MOFs improvesthe mechanical and electrical properties of the single-phase MOFs, while also introducing a variety of new functionalities [24,25]. Researchinterest in this has been significant, culminating in composites including MOF-porous carbon, MOF-carbon nanotube, MOF-graphite or graphene oxide, MOF-carbon fiber, and MOF-carbon dots [26–28]. Biomass-derived CD functionalized MOF composites leverage both components' strengths, broadening their potential application areas [25]. Due to excellent biodegradability, low cost, sustainability,and structural and functional customization capability, biomass materials derived carbon dots have gained much interest over the last few years [29,30].

Cumin (Cuminum cyminum L.), a biennial herbof the Apiaceae family, is an annually harvested dried fruit. They areyellowish-brown and aromatic with a distinctive piquant, bitter, and intense flavor [31]. The principal constituent of cumin is cumin aldehyde, which is found in its essential oil. Cumin offers diverse applications, encompassing food, drinks, pharmaceuticals, hygiene, and fragrances. It is a necessary component of curry powder and spice blends, utilized to enhance the flavor of soups, vegetables, sausages, pickles, and other curries. Moreover, itserves as a fungicide, insecticide and veterinary medicine [31,32]. Building on the multifunctional advantages of cumin, this work focuses on utilizing cumin as a sustainable carbon source to produce carbon dots.

This research provides valuable insights into the potential of modified iron MOFs as next-generation corrosion inhibitor, emphasizing both performance and sustainability. Herein, we report the synthesis of a biomass-derived carbon dots-embedded metal-organic framework (MOF@CD) composite. After successfully characterizing the morphology, functional groups and thermal stability of the formed MOF@CD NC usingScanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), UV–vis and thermogravimetric (TG) analysis, the MOF@CD NC was further applied as a novel corrosion inhibitor of Q235B steel in acidic media. Chemical, electrochemical andsurface studies showed that it has the potential to be used as a sustainable and efficient protective material to protect internal pipeline corrosion. The functionalization of MOFs with carbon dots is an innovative strategy that provides improved adsorption properties and corrosion protection, which has not been explored in prior literature.

Ferric Chloride (≥98.5 %) was purchased from Fujian Keshi Chemical Co., Ltd, (China). Terephthalic acid (TPA, 99.6 %) was purchased from Bide Pharmaceutical, and Dimethyl formamide (DMF, 99.5 %) was obtained from Aladdin Scientific (China). Cumin seeds were purchased from a local grocery store (U.P., India). The chemical composition of cumin seed extract was reported to be [33] and is given in Table 1:
The MOF@CD NC was synthesized in a three-step process involving the preparation of the iron-based MOF, CDs, and their subsequent hybridization.

Step 1: Synthesis of iron-based MOF. An iron-based MOF was synthesized via a solvothermal method [34,35]. First, 1.34 g ofFeCl₃·6H₂O was dissolved in 10 mL of DMF, while a separate solution was prepared by dissolving 0.412 g of TPA in 30 mL of DMF. The two solutions were mixed and moved toan autoclave reactor of stainless steel. The reaction was performed at 110 °C for 20 h, after which the reaction wascooled to room temperature, and the orange precipitate was obtained by centrifugation and washed three times with ethanol. The washed product was dried at 60 °C under vacuum for 12 h to yield Fe-MOF.

Step 2: Synthesis of CDs: CDs were prepared using the hydrothermal method [36]. Cumin seeds were cleaned, air dried at 25 °C, and groundto a fine powder. A sample of 1.0 g powdered cumin was dispersed into 50 mL deionized water and transferred to a100 mL stainless steel autoclave. The mixture was heated at 190 °C for 7 h. After cooling down the solution to room temperature, the reaction mixture was centrifuged twice at 8000 rpm for 15 mins to isolate the supernatant containing CDs. The supernatant was then filtered through a Whatman nylon membrane filter (13 mm diameter, 0.22 µm pore size), dialyzed for 24 h at 25 °C in deionized water using a 3500 Da MWCO membrane. The resulting clear yellowish-brown CD solution was stored at 4 °C for subsequent use.

Step 3: Synthesis of MOF@CD NC: CD solution wasmixed with MOF solution with a weight ratio of 2:1 to synthesize MOF@CD NC [34]. In general, 50 mL of CDs solution(7.3 mg/mL) was mixed with 50 mL of MOF solution (3.65 mg/mL). The mixture was stirred at 25 °C for 24 h and the NC was obtained through centrifuging at 8000 rpm for10 mins, followed by drying the precipitate at 70 °C overnight to obtain the MOF@CD NC as a powdered product.
Fig. 1 shows the schematic representation of MOF@CD synthesis.

The MOF@CD's X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was recorded using a Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (40 kV, 40 mA) at a scan rate of 6°/min across the 2θ range of 10° to 90°. For Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, lyophilized MOF@CD NC was mixed with dry KBr, pressed into a pellet under 40 MPa pressure for 2 minutes using a hydraulic press, and analyzed with a Nicolet IS10 instrument (USA) over the spectral range of 4000–400 cm⁻¹ . Thermogravimetric (TG) analysis of MOF@CD was performed using a NETZSCH STA 449 F3/F5 Jupiter thermal analyzer in an N2 atmosphere. Approximately 6 mg sample was heated at a rate of 10 °C/min over 30–900°C. UV–vis absorption spectra were collected with a Thermo Scientific Evolution 300 spectrophotometer with quartz cuvettes (2 mL capacity, 10 mm optical path length). The scans were performed at a resolution of 1.0 nm over the wavelength range of 200–700 nm. The material's morphology was examined with a scanning electron microscope (SEM, JSM-7800F). Elemental mapping analysis was conducted utilizing the integrated energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) unit of SEM.

Steel samples were prepared by mechanically cutting Q235B steel plates into dimensions of 3 cm × 2.0 cm × 1 cm. The chemical composition of the steel can be found in our previous publications [37,38]. Each sample was abraded using emery papers of varying grit sizes (#100 to #7000) to achieve a mirror-like surface. The polished samples undergo a cleaning process consisting of sonication in ethanol for 10 minutes, followed by acetone cleaning for 1 minute, rinsing with ethanol, and ending with rinsing using deionized (DI) water. The abraded samples were dried utilizing a drier. The acidic solution used in the investigation was formulated by diluting analytical-grade hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37 %) with deionized water to achieve a 1.0 M concentration.

Weight loss measurements were conducted in 250 mL beakers by immersing Q235B steel coupons in the testing solution for 6, 24, and 72 h, without and with MOF@CD NC at 25 °C and 50 °C. Before immersion in both control and inhibited solutions, the Q235B steel coupons were cleaned, polished, and weighed with an electronic balance having a sensitivity of ± 0.0001 g. To assess the impact of MOF@CD NC on Q235B steel in a 1.0 M HCl solution, the coupons were removed from the solution and subsequently cleaned to eliminate corrosion products [39], and reweighed. The weight loss data were derived from the average of three samples, and the mean value was utilized to compute the inhibitory efficiency. The corrosion rate (CR, mm/y) inhibition efficiency (%η WL) was calculated using Eqs. 1 and (2) [40]:

where W is the weight loss in grams, t is the immersion time in h, A is the exposed surface area of the steel specimens in cm2, ρ is the density of Q235B steel, CR0 and CRi represent corrosion rates in the absence and presence of inhibitor, respectively.

Electrochemical experiments were performed in a standard three-electrode glass cell (250 mL), utilizing a CHI660E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instruments Co., Ltd). The configuration comprised a saturated Ag/AgCl reference electrode with a Luggin capillary, a platinum sheet as a counter electrode, and a Q235B steel as a working electrode. The steel samples were cut into 10 mm × 10 mm × 10 mm cubes for the preparation of the electrode. Then, the cubes were welded with wire, and the edges of the specimens were sealed with epoxy for electrochemical measurement exposing only a 1.0 cm2 working area. All potentials are referenced to the Ag/AgCl electrode. Before measurements, the working electrode was immersed in the test solution for approximately 30 mins to attain a stable open-circuit potential (OCP). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were conducted across a frequency spectrum of 100 kHz to 10 mHz at the OCP, utilizing a sinusoidal potential perturbation with an amplitude of 10 mV. The cell temperature was regulated at 25 °C and 50 °C ( ± 1 °C) with a thermostatic water bath. Data from the electrochemical tests were gathered and analyzed utilizing ZsimpWin software (version 3.3d). Polarization curves were obtained by scanning from a cathodic potential of −0.25 V to an anodic potential of + 0.25 V relative to OCP, at a sweep rate of 0.001 V/s. The linear Tafel segments of the anodic and cathodic polarization curves were extrapolated to the corrosion potential (E corr) to ascertain the corrosion current densities (I corr) [41]. Following formula was used to calculate the corrosion rates (CR) using I corr values:

Thermoelectric Thermoescalab 250XI was used for XPS research to establish the inhibitors' surface composition. Binding energies for the constituent elements were calibrated using C1s (284.8 eV) and Al-Kα radiation (1486.6 eV). The steel specimens were cleaned with acetone and deionized water before being placed in a pre-chamber for 15 h, after the samples were transferred to the analytical chamber, maintained at a base pressure of 1 × 10−9 Torr. XPS analyses were conducted after 120 seconds of Ar sputtering.

Surface morphological analysis was conducted to assess the impact of the inhibitor and its interaction with the substrate. Steel coupons were polished to a mirror-like finish using SiC emery papers of varying particle sizes, as discussed above, and immersed in 1.0 M HCl for 24 h and 72 h at 25 °C K and 50 °C, both with and without 100 mg/L MOF@CD NC. The same SEM instrument, as described above, was used for analysis. Also, the visual inspection of Q235B steel surfaces was recorded using a smartphone camera in the absence and presence of MOF@CD NC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface morphology and elemental composition: SEM, XRD and EDS mapping analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The SEM images of Fe-MOF and MOF@CD NC were captured at magnifications of 20,000 × and 60,000 × and the differences were substantial. As evidenced in panels a and b of Fig. 2, Fe-MOF particles demonstrate well-defined polyhedral shapes with discreteedges and smooth surfaces, featuring high crystallinity and uniform growth. The XRD pattern shown in Fig. 3(a) shows sharp and distinct multiple peaks, corresponding to 9.4°, 18.7°, 26.8°, 28.1°, 32.4°, 33.4°, 36.0°, 43.6°, 47.0° and 50.3° confirming the highly crystalline nature of Fe-MOF [42,43], which is in agreement with the geometric features observed in SEM.

In contrast, MOF@CD NC, shown in panels c and d in Fig. 2, demonstrates rounded structures with increased surface roughness due to CD modification. This indicatesthe adequate integration of CDs that lowers the crystallinity and increases the surface functionalization. XRD analysis also shows decreased intensity of peaks and broadenedreflections, suggesting loss of crystallinity. This is attributed to the incorporation of amorphous CDs, which interfere with the ordered structure of Fe-MOF [44]. According to the XRD pattern of MOF@CD NC nanocomposite, due to the loading of CDs in FeMOF, a peak for CDs at about 18.6° was detected which further confirms the incorporation of CDs in MOF. Fig. 3(b,c) shows elemental mapping of FeMOF and itsnanocomposite, revealing that both materials are predominantly composed of C, O and Fe. The successful doping of CDs in the nanocomposite structure can also be confirmed by thepresence of a small quantity of N element, which can be seen in the mapping image of MOF@CD NC (Fig. 3c).

FT-IR spectroscopic analysis is used to identify the functional groups in molecules, as specific chemical bonds typically exhibit unique energy absorption bands. The FT-IR spectra of pure FeMOF and MOF@CD NC ( Fig. 4a) show clear differences that suggest successful modification. In pure FeMOF, the O–H stretching vibration at 3379 cm⁻¹is related to hydroxyl groups, while the peaks at 3056 cm⁻¹ and 2923 cm⁻¹ correspond to C–H stretching. The carboxylate groups are demonstrated by the peaks between 1654 cm⁻¹ (CO stretching), 1565 cm⁻¹ (asymmetricCOO⁻ stretching) and 1385 cm⁻¹ (symmetric COO⁻ stretching). The other peaks observed at 1152 and 1085 cm⁻¹ are associated with C–Ostretching, whereas peaks at 822 and 738 cm⁻¹ belong to aromatic C–H bending vibrations. The coordination of Fe centers with oxygen atoms in the MOF frameworkis also identified by the Fe–O stretching vibration at 540 cm⁻¹ [34]. In the case of CD-modified FeMOF, the O–H stretching vibration shifts to 3421 cm⁻¹ and broadens slightly, which signifies that the hydrogen bonding was enhanced due to the introduction of hydroxyl groups of CDs. The C–H stretching shifts to 2912 cm⁻¹ with reduced intensity, reflecting the interaction of CDs with the MOF framework. The new peak observed at 1954 cm⁻¹ may be assigned to carbonyl or conjugated CO functional groups of the CDs. CC or COO⁻ stretching shifts slightly from 1565 cm⁻¹ to 1583 cm⁻¹ , indicating an interaction between the CDs and the aromatic or carboxylate groups. The disappearance of C–O stretching peaks at 1152 and 1085 cm⁻¹ , along with the emergence of a new peak at 1032 cm⁻¹ , further supports significant interactions between CDs and the MOF framework. Aromatic C–H bending vibrations shift to 876 cm⁻¹ and 745 cm⁻¹ , suggesting modifications in the aromatic environment due to the presence of CDs. The characteristic vibration of Fe–O stretching at 540 cm⁻¹ almost unchanged, which indicates that the core structure of FeMOF was reserved after modification[45]. Moreover, the UV spectra of MOF@CD are shown in Fig. 4(b), and it presents a peak of λ= 290 nm at the absorbance of 0.311. This indicates that the CD@MOF absorbs light in the UV region, which is probably ascribed to theelectronic transitions of CDs. As shown in the inset of Fig. 4(b), blue fluorescence was observed when MOF@CD was dispersed in water and illuminated with UV light at 365 nm.

TG measurements of Fe-MOF and MOF@CD NC were recorded in an N2 atmosphere with a heating rate of 10 °C/min. The TGA curves depicted in Fig. 4(c) indicates that there are three stages for the mass loss of the as-prepared Fe-MOF sample with temperature. The first mass loss in the range of128.8–231.24 °C is nearly 12.18 %, which is associated with the removal of adsorbed water and DMF [42]. The second significant mass loss (22.6 %) occursbetween 343 °C and 498 °C, which can be attributed to the decomposition of the oxygen-containing groups in the organic ligands of TPA and DMF of Fe-MOF. With the increasing temperature, the mass loss of step 3 from 420.0 °C to 538.6 °C may be attributed to the loss of chlorine, the decomposition of aromatic rings, and the conformation of iron oxides[46]. On the other hand, CD-modified FeMOF also undergoes a three-step decomposition process Fig. 4(c). Initially, the volatilization of adsorbed water occurs at around 100 °C. Beyond 250 °C, a sharp weight loss is observed due to the weakening of π-π interactions and the decomposition of -CO and -COOH groups in the CDs, which releases CO2 and H2O [47]. This sharp weight loss in the initial stage confirms the presence of CDs on the surface of FeMOF. However, as the temperature exceeds 280 °C, the weight loss in CD-modified FeMOF is somewhat lower compared to pure FeMOF (Fig. 4c). This reduction can be attributed to strong chemical interactions, such as π-π transitions and hydrogen bonding, between the CDs and the MOF framework [47]. Additionally, the structural reinforcement provided by the CDs occupying the MOF pores, along with their graphitic nature acting as a thermal stabilizer, enhances the interfacial adhesive forces and improves the thermal stability of the CD-modified FeMOF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Corrosion inhibition performance of MOF@CD NC observed by gravimetric analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 depicts the fluctuations in (a) corrosion rates and (b) inhibition efficiencies exhibited by MOF@CD NC for Q235B steel immersed in 1.0 M HCl solution, assessed over 24 and 72 h at 25 °C and 50 °C. The findings demonstrate that MOF@CD NC significantly diminishes the metal's degradation in an acidic environment. Fig. 5(a) illustrates that the corrosion rate reduces as the concentrations of MOF@CD NC increase, with the minimum rate recorded at 100 mg/L, thereby defining this concentration as optimum. Furthermore, at 25 °C, corrosion processes were more successfully mitigated following prolonged immersion (72 h) time in contrast with shorter immersion (24 h). This improvement is attributed to the gradual development of a thicker, more stable inhibitor layer that effectively hinders the ingress of corrosive ions [48,49]. Over time, enough MOF@CD NC molecules accumulate on the steel surface, forming a protective barrier that greatly reduces the corrosion rates. Similar findings have been previously reported in the literature [50]. It is noteworthy that the corrosion rates for 24 and 72 h at 25 °C fall within the acceptable range of 0.51–1.27 mm/yr (20–50 mpy) for steel in inhibited HCl solutions under atmospheric or deaerated conditions [51].

Fig. 5(b) reveals three distinct trends in inhibition efficiency: (i) the inhibition efficiency increased with immersion time, reaching a maximum at 72 h at 25 °C, likely due to an enhanced adsorption of MOF@CD NC molecules on the steel surface [52] (ii) at 50 °C, after 24 h, the inhibition efficiency improved, indicating that higher temperatures may facilitate stronger or faster adsorption of the inhibitor and (iii) the inhibition efficiency slightly declined with extended immersion times at 50 °C, which might be attributed to partial desorption of inhibitor molecules from the surface [53]. A negative inhibition efficiency (-15.01 %) was observed at 10 mg/L of MOF@CD NC at 50 °C after 72 h immersion, as shown in Fig. 5(b). This suggests that at such low concentration and prolonged immersion (72 h), MOF@CD NC fails to provide adequate corrosion protection. However, after the immersion of Q235B steel for 72 h in 100 mg/L MOF@CD NC, the %IE was observed to be 87.8 %. These results underline the importance of optimizing both the concentration and immersion duration to maximize the performance of MOF@CD NC as a corrosion inhibitor.

Langmuir isotherm was used to provide valuable insights into the mechanism of adsorption and the surface behavior of organic molecules. The model is expressed mathematically as follows: The corresponding figure illustration is shown in Fig. 6 [54].
(6)

=
1

ads
+ C
where θ is the surface coverage, C is the inhibitor concentration, and K ads is the equilibrium adsorption constant.

The graph depicted in Fig. 6, illustrating that the C/θ versus C at 25 and 50 °C, exhibits a linear relationship. This linearity and the values of the R² (0.9992 and 0.9999) suggest that the adsorption of inhibitor molecules aligns closely with the Langmuir adsorption model suggesting that the adsorption of inhibitor molecules on the Q235B steel surface is classified as monomolecular layer adsorption. The calculated K ads values of MOF@CD NC at 50 °C (0.72) is higher as compared to the values obtained at 25 °C (0.25) emphasizing the strong affinity between the inhibitor molecules and Q235B steel substrate surface [55]. The adsorption equilibrium constant is related to Gibbs free energy of adsorption (ΔG ° ads) according to Eq. 7 [56]:
(7)

)
R is the gas constant (8.314 J mol–1 K–1), and T is the absolute temperature in Kelvin and 1 × 106 is the concentration of water in solution in ppm.

The calculated negative values of ΔG ° ads (-30.82 and −35.10 kJ/mol) at both the temperatures indicates a strong interaction between the inhibitor molecules and the metal surface, suggesting the spontaneity of the adsorption process [56].

OCP test characterizes protective film stability by monitoring voltage changes over time. E ocp vs time curves are presented in Fig. S1 (supplementary information). As shown in Fig. S1 (supplementary information), the E OCP curves of Q235B steel in 1.0 M HCl stabilizes after 487 s at 25 °C and 954 s at 50 °C, indicating equilibrium [57]. At 25 °C, the E OCP values shifts from –0.462 V (uninhibited) to fewer negative values of –0.448 V (100 mg/L, 50 mg/L) and –0.452 V (30 mg/L, 10 mg/L) with increasing MOF@CD NC concentration, demonstrating a reduction in anodic reaction and suggesting MOF@CD NC acts as an anodic inhibitor. Conversely, at 50 °C, E OCP values shifts to more negative values, indicating enhanced cathodic reaction suppression and highlighting MOF@CD NC’s role as a mixed-type inhibitor. These observations suggest MOF@CD NC effectively delays Fe oxide and hydroxide formation by forming a stable inhibitor-steel complex, preventing further corrosive reactions in acidic conditions.

EIS is a widely adopted technique for assessing the corrosion inhibition efficacy of inhibitors. Fig. 7(a-d) illustrates the Nyquist and Bode plots of Q235B steel immersed in acidic media, both without and with various concentrations of MOF@CD NC at 25 and 50 °C following 24 h immersion.

Fig. 7(a,c) presents Nyquist diagrams that exhibit a significant capacitive semi-circle at elevated frequencies and a smaller inductive loop at reduced frequencies across all samples. An increase in the diameter of the capacitive semi-circles is observed with increasing MOF@CD NC concentration, following the order: 100 mg/L > 50 mg/L > 30 mg/L > 10 mg/L > blank. This trend signifies enhanced corrosion resistance of Q235B steel, especially with 100 mg/L MOF@CD NC, indicating strong inhibitor adsorption on the steel surface. The high-frequency capacitive semi-circle is associated with the charge transfer reaction. In contrast, the low-frequency inductive loop is ascribed to the adsorption and diffusion processes of H ads or [(FeOH)ads] during the anode dissolution of the steel [58]. The equivalent circuit shown in Fig. 7(e) was used to analyze EIS data, and the results are summarized in Table2. The fitted equivalent circuit model comprises solution resistance (R s), polarization resistance (R p), and film resistance (R f) with R f denoting the total resistance (R t = R p+ R f) [58,59]. Constant phase elements (CPE f and CPE dl) account for the non-ideal capacitive response of the double layer and surface film, respectively [60]. The parameter n quantifies variations of the CPE from ideal capacitance, influenced by surface permeability and particle dispersion. The chi-squared statistic (χ 2), determined to be in the range of 10–4, signifies a good alignment between the experimental data and the suggested model [61]. Moreover, R p values exhibited a distinct association with the rising concentrations of MOF@CD NC, indicating improved protective efficacy at increased concentrations.

The inhibition efficiencies increased with MOF@CD NC concentrations, reaching a peak of 94.1 % at 100 mg/L, highlighting the inhibitor's excellent corrosion inhibition capacity. Furthermore, CPE dl values diminished steadily with increasing concentrations of MOF@CD NC due to the displacement of water molecules at the metal/solution interface by inhibitor molecules [62]. The substitution resulted in establishing a protective MOF@CD NC covering the steel surface, successfully protecting Q235B steel from corrosion under acidic conditions.

Fig. 7(b,d) show Bode impedance and phase angle plots for steel immersed in different concentration of MOF@CD NC. A higher impedance modulus at the lowest frequency (|Z|0.01, Hz) signifies better corrosion inhibition. The results demonstrate that the addition of MOF@CD NC results in a significant increase in |Z|0.01 values and the highest value was observed at 100 mg/L. In the Bode-phase angle plots, the phase angles increased and exhibited broader plateaus between 102 −104 Hz range upon increasing MOF@CD NC concentrations, indicating strong adsorption of the corrosion inhibitor [63]. These results obtained from EIS measurements closely corresponded with the outcomes of the weight losstests, proving that the MOF@CD NC acted as an efficient corrosion inhibitor.

Fig. 8(a–d) presents the potentiodynamic polarization curves of Q235B steel in 1.0 M HCl at 25 °C and 50 °C after 24 and 72 h immersion with varying concentrations of MOF@CD NC. The electrochemical parameters, i.e., corrosion current density (I corr), corrosion potential (E corr), anodic Tafel slope (β a) and cathodic Tafel slope (β c), derived from these plots are summarized in Table 3. Table 3 indicates that when the concentration of MOF@CD NC increases, the reduction in I corr values occurred. The I corr value in uninhibited solution was observed to be 408.8 µA/cm2 which decreased to 20.2 µA/cm2 in presence of optimum concentration i.e., 100 mg/L of MOF@CD NC at 50 °C after 24 h immersion, confirming the effectiveness of MOF@CD NC at higher concentrations and temperature. Although E corr values shifted less than 85 mV (Table 3) indicate that increasing the concentration of the MOF@CD NC inhibitor results in a less negative shift in corrosion potential relative to the uninhibited solution. MOF@CD NC seems to act as a mixed-type inhibitor [64,65] by reducing the anodic reaction (Fe → Fe2⁺ + 2e⁻) and the cathodic reaction (2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂). The result indicates that MOF@CD NC plays a shielding role in corrosion while inhibiting the penetration of corrosive ions. From Fig. 8, it can be inferred that the anodic and cathodic current–potential curves are almost parallel lines, indicating that the MOF@CD NC inhibitor does not significantly alter the fundamental mechanism or kinetics of any corrosion reaction [66,67]. The small differences in β a and β c suggest that the inhibitor influences both anodic dissolution of iron and cathodic hydrogen evolution without affecting the fundamental electrochemical mechanisms.

Fig. 8(c-d) illustrates the potentiodynamic polarization curves for Q235B steel following 72 h of immersion in 1.0 M HCl solution, both in the absence and presence of 10 mg/L and 100 mg/L of MOF@CD NC at 25 °C and 50 °C. The presence of MOF@CD NC in the acidic solution significantly reduced both anodic and cathodic current densities, indicating mixed corrosion inhibition [18]. The presence of MOF@CD NC results in a reduction of the current density, with no appreciable shift in E corr, indicating the adsorption of these molecules onto the metallic surface. Moreover, the configuration of the anodic and cathodic branches appears unchanged compared to the blank solution. Moreover, at elevated temperature (50 °C), the potentiodynamic polarization curve recorded for 100 mg/L of the MOF@CD inhibitor exhibits a distinct cathodic current plateau. This behavior signifies the formation of a highly protective inhibitor film that effectively limits the access of hydrogen ions to cathodic sites, thereby suppressing the hydrogen evolution reaction.

As shown in Fig. 9(a), the UV–visspectrum of MOF@CD NC has a peak at 290 nm. The absorption spectrum ofthe solution of Q235B steel immersed in 1.0 M HCl containing different concentrations (10, 30, 50 and 100 mg/L) of MOF@CD NC showed a peak at 332 nm after 24 h immersion. The shift of the absorption peak can be ascribed to the interaction of the MOF@CD NC, whichis closely related to the π-π* electronic transition of the aromatic or conjugated moiety in the backbone of the cumin-derived CDs. This behaviorindicated low aggregation and stable interaction of the steel surface with the MOF@CD NC. The nitrogen and oxygen atoms adsorb onto the surface of steel which potentially is a shielding film hence leading to corrosion inhibition. The stability of this peak denotes the corrosioninhibition efficiency of CDs, performance retained with concentration [68].

FT-IR spectra of MOF@CD NC adsorbed on a steel substratein 1.0 M HCl solution have been studied (Fig. 9b). FT-IR spectra of adsorbed MOF@CD NC on metal surface show obviousdifference with pure MOF@CD NC (Fig. 4a),that reflect adsorption interactions. Enhanced hydrogen bonding is inferred from the broad O-H stretching peak at 3437 cm⁻¹ , whereas slight shifts in C-H stretching peaks (2920 and 2838 cm⁻¹) indicate retained alkyl groups. The peak intensities at 1602 and 1497 cm⁻¹ which correspond to CO and aromatic CC stretching suggest coordination with the metalsurface and π- electron interaction. The shiftof the C-O peaks (1184 cm⁻¹) suggests the participation of oxygen species groups in adsorption. The aromatic C-H bending peak at 824 cm⁻¹ is has only minor changes, confirming that the aromatic structure remains stable [34]. Finally, the metal-O stretching peak at 460 cm⁻¹verifies the integrity of the MOF framework after adsorption [69]. The results indicate that while the structure skeleton is preserved, functional groups such as hydroxyl,and carbonyl coordinate the metal surface on adsorption.

XPS analysis reveals the chemical composition andbond states of surface elements. Fig. 10(a-d) presentsthe chemical composition of the steel surfaces after immersion for 24 h in HCl electrolyte, with and without 100 mg/L of MOF@CD NC. The survey plots of the adsorbed elements are shownin Fig. 10(a). The C1s spectrum (Fig. 10b) displays apparent peaks found at 284.6 eV, 298.9 eV, 287.3 eV, and 288.4 eV corresponding to C−C/C−H, C−N/CN, and CO, respectively [27]. O 1 s spectrum(Fig. 10c) displays three prominent peaks at 531.1 eV, 532.5 eV and 533.7 eVcorresponding respectively to O-H or Fe-O coordination, C-O and CO [70]. On the metal surface, thesefeatures indicate oxides and hydroxides, resulting from the interaction of the acid-inhibited solution with the steel substrate. Such interaction results in theformation of a protective layer, which ultimately improves the inhibition efficiency by blocking more active sites for corrosion. N1s spectra shown in Fig. 10(d) exhibited three peaks at 399.65 eV, 400.45 eV and 401.3 eV corresponding to C–N, N-Fe, and N+H bonds. Four peaks were observed inthe XPS spectrum of Fe 2p in Fig. 10(e), at about 710, 713, and 724, 725 eV. The peaks at ∼ 710 eV and ∼724 eV are ascribed to the Fe 2p₃/₂ and Fe 2p₁/₂ features of Fe²⁺, respectively, while those at ∼713 eV and ∼725 eV correspondto the Fe 2p₃/₂ and Fe 2p₁/₂ features of Fe³ ⁺, which are in agreement with previous reports [71].

These oxides indicate that the corrosion phenomenon has produced alayer of complex iron oxides with different oxidation states that are likely to be involved in the adsorption of the inhibitor and its protection process.

AFM study wasconducted to study the surface morphology of both blank and inhibited Q235B steel samples, which reflect the corrosion damage and protection efficiency of MOF@CD NC. In Fig. 11(a), the blank steel surface exhibited rugged, mountain-like topography along with significant amount of corrosion product formation with average roughness (R a) and root mean square roughness (R q) values of 87.8 nm and 113 nm, respectively.

The rough surface is a clear indication of the rapid corrosion of the unprotected steel surface in the acidic solution, withextensive dissolution of metal. Fig. 11(b), on the otherhand, shows the surface of the inhibited steel sample. Traces of corrosion products are missing, and the steel surface is smooth. R a and R q were25.7 nm and 33.1 nm, respectively. The comparativesurface morphologies between blank and inhibited samples confirm the efficacy of the inhibitor in corrosion inhibition. This forms a barrier and shields the surface of the metal from the corrosive medium and protects the integrity ofthe metal. This work suggests that MOF@CD NC is a promising corrosion inhibitor for Q235B steel in an acidic medium.

As presented in Fig. 12(a-h), SEM was usedto study the surface morphology of Q235B steel at different conditions. The micrographs show the influence of immersion time and temperature on the surface morphology of Q235B steel in 1.0 M HCl without and with 100 mg/L of MOF@CD NC at different temperatures and immersion times. Fig. 12(a), c, e, and gshow the steel surface after immersion for 24 and 72 h in 1.0 M HCl at 25 °C and 50 °C, respectively, in the absence of the inhibitor. Theimages show surface deterioration, including cracks, cavities and irregular morphology. These characteristics emphasize the intense corrosive action of the acid environment onthe exposed metal. Without MOF@CD NC, the metal is susceptible, with corrosion products and damageemerging clearly. Fig. 12(b), d, f, and h illustrate the steel surface image however with 100 mg/L MOF@CD NC addition. The inhibited samples on the other hand arein stark contrast with their smooth surfaces and very little apparent damage. As shown in the results, the protective layer inhibits the direct contactbetween the steel and the corrosive medium. The metal holdsits structure with the surface intact.

The order showed that the performance of the inhibitor is followed by the level of smoothness: after 24 h of immersion at 50 °C > after 72 h of immersion at 25 °C > after 24 h of immersion at 25 °C > after 72 h of immersion at 50 °C, which indicates that after 24 h of immersion at 50 °C, the inhibitor has the best performance of inhibiting corrosion. However, the degree of surface smoothness decreases significantly at 72 h of immersion and higher temperatures(50 °C), suggesting that the protective layer formed by MOF@CD NC is time-dependent and that the corrosion-resistant ability of MOF@CD NC decreases at longer immersion time.

The corrosionbehavior of Q235B steel is also corroborated by visual observations. Fig. S2 (supplementary information) revealed that the steel surface exposed to the uninhibited acidic solution was severely corroded, with a blackened surface associated with the aggressive acidicenvironment, resulting in substantial degradation. In contrast, the steel surface immersed in the presence of MOF@CD NC showed almost no corrosion, which indicated that the inhibitor was adsorbed on the metal surface, leading to providing adequate protection. These visual effects support the results of electrochemical and the weight loss measurements,demonstrating that the MOF@CD NC inhibitor successfully reduced corrosion in the acidic environment as compared to uninhibited steel.

The corrosion inhibition mechanism of the MOF@CD NC is largely driven by the synergistic effects between the Fe-MOF and the bioactive compounds present in cumin seed derived CDs. The prepared CDs contains a variety of phytoconstituents as listed in Table 1. These constituents contribute uniquely to surface adsorption and film formation. For example, cumin aldehyde, the major compound, contains a conjugated aldehyde group that can form coordinate bonds with the metal surface. Compounds like p-cymene and γ-terpinene possess aromatic rings that promote π–π interactions with the metal substrate. Meanwhile, perilla aldehyde and cuminyl alcohol offer polar functional groups (–CHO and –OH), which can engage in hydrogen bonding or coordinate with iron atoms. When these functionalized CDs are used to modify Fe-MOF, the resulting MOF@CD NC combines the high surface area and porosity of the MOF with the surface-active functionalities of the CDs. Upon immersion in corrosive media, the NC adsorbs onto the steel surface via multiple mechanisms, including π–π interactions from aromatic compounds, hydrogen bonding or coordination from polar groups, and chemisorption through lone pair electron donation from nitrogen and oxygen atoms. This cooperative interaction leads to the formation of a stable, multicomponent protective film that effectively blocks aggressive ions and reduces corrosion by impeding both anodic and cathodic reactions. Also, the synergistic interactions among these compounds enhance surface coverage and inhibition efficiency, with aromatic molecules (e.g., p-cymene) promoting π–π interactions, and polar molecules (e.g., cumin aldehyde) establishing coordination with metal atoms. The presence of nitrogen and oxygen elements in the XPS confirms the effective adsorption of inhibitor molecules and the formation of a stable corrosion-protective layer on the metal surface.

This work prepared CD-modified metal-organic framework and studied their efficacy against the Q235B steel corrosion in a 1.0 M HCl electrolyte. The inhibition efficiency of MOF@CD NC at 100 mg/L was as highas 98.05 %, indicating its outstanding performance. Furthermore, the acid corrosion inhibition mechanism of MOF@CD NC wasstudied by polarization analysis, which showed that MOF@CD NC serves as a mixed-type inhibitor that protects against both anodic and cathodic corrosion processes. The improved anticorrosive performance canbe attributed to the formation of a thin protective layer of MOF@CD NC on the steel surface, as demonstrated by EIS measurements. We corroborated the apparent chemical nature of the adsorption of MOF@CD NC on the metal surface based on the results from XPS and FT-IR analysis. These analyses revealed polar functional groups, such as C-O and O-H functional groups, present in a protective film on thesurface of steel. This result indicates thatthe chemical interaction of the inhibitor with the steel surface affects the stability and integrity of the protective layer. The electrochemical and spectroscopic results were also supported by surface morphology evaluation with SEM, AFM and visual observation. The steel substrate had a dense anduniform "protective film" due to the adsorption of MOF@CD NC. Such corrosion inhibition results from the inability of the aggressive acidic medium to access the steel substrate through the screening layer of the MOF@CD NC, revealing that MOF@CD NC performed as a high-performance and sustainablecorrosion inhibitor.
